original_hypothesis.title: onlyOperator Modifier Inverted Logic Locks Out All Designated Operators
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The onlyOperator modifier uses `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert` which reverts when operator[msg.sender] is true. This means designated operators can NEVER call harvest, delegateAmount, undelegateAmount, redelegateAmount, harvestAndDelegateRewards, or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. Additionally, if the owner is also set as an operator via updateOperator(owner, true), even the owner is permanently locked out of all operator functions until the mapping is toggled back.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The boolean condition `msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]` evaluates to true (reverts) whenever operator[msg.sender] is true, regardless of ownership. The intended logic is clearly `msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]` — allowing either the owner OR an operator to proceed. This is a critical access control defect affecting all staking management functions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — OR operator should use AND with negation: != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]

preconditions: An address is set as operator via updateOperator(addr, true)
That operator attempts to call any onlyOperator-guarded function

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect boolean logic in access control modifier
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: operator[msg.sender] being true causes revert instead of allowing access
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No test coverage for operator-as-non-owner calling gated functions

analysis_questions: Does the deployment script set any operators? (No — only owner deploys and calls directly)
Can the owner still call operator functions? (Yes, only if operator[owner] is false)
If owner calls updateOperator(owner, true), are all operator functions bricked?
validation_requirements: Test: set operator[addr]=true, have addr call harvest() — expect revert
Test: set operator[owner]=true, have owner call delegateAmount() — expect revert
Verify no alternate code path bypasses the modifier

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: After deployment, if the owner sets a bot as operator and assumes the bot handles harvesting/finalization, all staking management stops. User withdrawal requests via requestWithdrawal cannot be finalized. The owner must directly manage all operations without being flagged as operator.
prerequisites: Owner sets at least one operator address via updateOperator

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total protocol paralysis if owner is also set as operator; delegation of staking ops impossible otherwise
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users — staking cannot be managed by designated operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can toggle operator[owner]=false to regain access, but designated operators remain permanently locked out

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that non-owner operators can call operator-gated functions

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Immediate on any operator function call

validation_result.mechanism: Inverted boolean logic in access control modifier
validation_result.impact_summary: The onlyOperator modifier reverts for all designated operators set via updateOperator(addr, true), making it impossible for non-owner operators to call harvest, delegate, undelegate, redelegate, harvestAndDelegateRewards, or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. If the owner is also set as an operator, even the owner is locked out.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The onlyOperator modifier at LiquidRon.sol:97-100 uses the condition `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator()`. The `||` (OR) operator makes the condition true (reverts) whenever EITHER sub-condition is true. For a designated operator (non-owner with operator[addr]=true): `msg.sender != owner()` is true, so the whole condition is true and it reverts — the second operand doesn't even matter. For the owner with operator[owner]=true: `msg.sender != owner()` is false, but `operator[msg.sender]` is true, so `false || true` = true and it reverts. Only the owner who is NOT in the operator mapping can pass the modifier (false || false = false). The correct logic should be `if (msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]) revert`, which would allow either the owner OR a designated operator to pass.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The modifier code at LiquidRon.sol:97-100 is unambiguously wrong. The boolean truth table proves that operator[msg.sender]=true always causes a revert regardless of ownership status. The deployment script at LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol confirms the owner calls delegateAmount directly (without being set as operator), which only works because operator[owner] defaults to false. No updateOperator call exists in the deployment script, meaning the protocol has never tested with actual designated operators.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The condition uses || (OR) which means: revert if caller is NOT owner OR if caller IS an operator. For any address where operator[addr]=true, the second disjunct is true, so the entire condition is true and the function reverts. The correct condition should be: if (msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator();

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls updateOperator(botAddr, true) → operator[botAddr] = true
3. onlyOperator modifier evaluates: msg.sender != owner() → true (botAddr is not owner)
4. Short-circuit OR: true || <anything> = true → revert ErrInvalidOperator()
5. Even evaluating fully: true || true = true → revert
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Any call to harvest(), harvestAndDelegateRewards(), delegateAmount(), redelegateAmount(), undelegateAmount(), or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() by a designated operator (operator[addr]=true, addr != owner) will always revert at the onlyOperator modifier (LiquidRon.sol:98). The modifier condition `msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]` evaluates to `true || true` = true for operators, triggering the revert. For the owner with operator[owner]=true, it evaluates to `false || true` = true, also reverting.

deployment_assumptions_checked: No operators are set in the deployment script
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol does not call updateOperator() at all. The deployer (owner) calls delegateAmount() directly, which works because operator[owner] defaults to false (Case: false || false = false, no revert). This confirms the bug has not been triggered in deployment but will manifest as soon as any operator is designated.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack scenario but a functional defect. After deployment, when the owner calls updateOperator(botAddress, true) to designate a staking management bot, that bot will be unable to call any operator-gated function. All calls from the bot to harvest(), delegateAmount(), finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch(), etc. will revert with ErrInvalidOperator(). If the owner additionally calls updateOperator(ownerAddress, true) (e.g., thinking they need to register themselves as operator too), even the owner loses access to all operator functions. The protocol's staking management, reward harvesting, and withdrawal finalization become completely inoperable until operator[owner] is toggled back to false.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. All six operator-gated functions are affected: harvest, harvestAndDelegateRewards, delegateAmount, redelegateAmount, undelegateAmount, and finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. Designated operators can never call these functions. If the owner is also set as operator, the entire protocol is bricked — no staking management, no reward harvesting, and no withdrawal finalization. User funds in requestWithdrawal become permanently locked since finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch cannot be called. The workaround is for the owner to operate directly without being in the operator mapping, but this defeats the purpose of operator delegation.
recommended_mitigations: Change the modifier to: if (msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator();
Add comprehensive unit tests verifying that non-owner operators can call all operator-gated functions
Add a test verifying that the owner who is also an operator can still call operator-gated functions
Add a test verifying that non-owner non-operator addresses are correctly rejected
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidRon in a Foundry test. 2. As owner, call updateOperator(operatorAddr, true). 3. As operatorAddr, call harvest() — expect revert with ErrInvalidOperator. 4. As owner, call updateOperator(owner, true). 5. As owner, call harvest() — expect revert with ErrInvalidOperator. 6. As owner, call updateOperator(owner, false). 7. As owner, call harvest() — expect success.
additional_testing_needed: Verify that no alternative code path bypasses the onlyOperator modifier for operator functions
Test all six affected functions with both operator and owner-as-operator scenarios
